critic_raw:
This design compares ResNet-50 against logistic regression on handcrafted features for diabetic retinopathy detection from retinal images.

The normalization is stated as computed from 'full dataset statistics prior to splitting.' Section 3 explicitly states per-channel mean and standard deviation are computed from the full dataset before splitting. This is the same leakage pattern present in multiple other designs: test-set pixel distributions contaminate training normalization. For retinal imaging, camera model and acquisition protocol differences between clinics can create systematic pixel-level distributional differences. If one clinic contributes disproportionately to the test set, normalizing against the full-dataset mean and standard deviation will encode that clinic's pixel characteristics into the normalization parameters applied during training.

Second, the primary metric is 'sensitivity at fixed specificity of ≥0.95.' The threshold is chosen on the validation set to achieve this specificity target and then applied to the test set. For a dataset of 50,000 images from multiple clinics with stratified temporal splits, the optimal threshold for achieving 95% specificity on the validation cohort (months 15-18) may not produce 95% specificity on the test cohort (months 19-24) if the class balance or feature distribution shifts. The design does not describe what happens if the pre-specified threshold misses the 95% specificity target on the test set — whether the reported sensitivity is still valid or whether the comparison is invalidated.

Third, the comparison between ResNet-50 (pretrained ImageNet) and logistic regression on 5 hand-crafted features is extremely asymmetric. The CNN has access to 512x512 pixel images with learned features across millions of pretrained parameters; the LR has access to 5 scalar values derived from rule-based image processing. The design claims this 'ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of learned representations' but what it actually isolates is the combined benefit of: (1) pretrained ImageNet features, (2) CNN capacity, and (3) access to all pixel information vs. 5 extracted statistics. An intermediate baseline (e.g., ResNet-50 features via global average pooling, frozen, with logistic regression head) would isolate the transfer learning benefit independently.

Fourth, the statistical test cited for comparing the two models' sensitivities is 'McPetar's test.' This is not a standard statistical test in the ML or medical imaging literature. The McNemar test is the standard paired test for comparing binary classifiers on the same test set, and the design likely intends this. Citing a non-existent test ('McPetar's') undermines the statistical validation protocol.
all_issues_raised: Image normalization statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting; test-set pixel distributions contaminate training normalization, and for multi-clinic datasets with systematic camera differences, this encodes test-clinic characteristics into training features.

The threshold for achieving 95% specificity is set on the validation cohort and applied to the test cohort, but class balance and feature distribution shifts across temporal splits may cause the pre-specified threshold to miss the 95% specificity target on the test set; the design does not specify how to handle this violation.

The comparison is between ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet (full pixel access, millions of parameters) and logistic regression on 5 scalar hand-crafted features; this is not a controlled comparison of learned vs. hand-crafted representations but a combined effect of pretraining, capacity, and information richness.

The statistical test cited as 'McPetar's test' does not exist; the standard paired classifier comparison test is McNemar's test, and citing a non-existent procedure undermines the validity of the statistical evaluation protocol.
all_issues_adjudicated: Image normalization statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting; test-set pixel distributions contaminate training normalization, and for multi-clinic datasets with systematic camera differences, this encodes test-clinic characteristics into training features.

The threshold for achieving 95% specificity is set on the validation cohort and applied to the test cohort, but class balance and feature distribution shifts across temporal splits may cause the pre-specified threshold to miss the 95% specificity target on the test set; the design does not specify how to handle this violation.

The comparison is between ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet (full pixel access, millions of parameters) and logistic regression on 5 scalar hand-crafted features; this is not a controlled comparison of learned vs. hand-crafted representations but a combined effect of pretraining, capacity, and information richness.

The statistical test cited as 'McPetar's test' does not exist; the standard paired classifier comparison test is McNemar's test, and citing a non-existent procedure undermines the validity of the statistical evaluation protocol.
